text.title: rag-architecture Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for rag-architecture. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Understand the two-phase RAG pipeline: indexing (documents → chunking → embedding → vector store) and query (query → embedding → retrieval → context → LLM → response).
rule.rationale: This separation enables independent scaling and debugging of ingestion vs. retrieval. Chunking decisions affect retrieval quality; embedding model selection affects search precision; vector database choice affects latency and cost.

rule.statement: Pipeline stages are: ingestion (load documents), chunking (split into pieces), embedding (vectorize chunks), indexing (store vectors), retrieval (find relevant chunks), generation (produce response). Each stage has key decisions: file types/sources, strategy/size/overlap, model/dimensions, index type, top-k/filtering, model/prompt design.
rule.rationale: Documenting key decisions at each stage enables teams to reason about tradeoffs and communicate constraints.

rule.statement: Fixed-size chunking: use for general text, mixed content. Pros: simple, predictable. Cons: may split context. Settings: 500 chars, 50 overlap.
rule.rationale: Fixed-size is the easiest to implement and understand, but produces arbitrary boundaries that can split semantic units.

rule.statement: Sentence-based chunking: use for narrative text, articles. Pros: natural boundaries. Cons: variable size. Settings: max 500 chars, min 100 chars.
rule.rationale: Sentences are natural semantic units. Size variance can lead to context overflow if queries retrieve many small chunks.

rule.statement: Paragraph-based chunking: use for structured documents. Pros: semantic units. Cons: large variance. Settings: max 1000 chars.
rule.rationale: Paragraphs are intentional semantic units written by authors; larger chunks reduce variance but may exceed context limits.

rule.statement: Semantic chunking: use for high-quality retrieval. Pros: context-aware splits. Cons: slow, expensive. Settings: 0.8 similarity threshold.
rule.rationale: Semantic chunking uses embeddings to find natural boundaries, but requires encoding each document during indexing, doubling compute cost.

rule.statement: Header-based (Markdown) chunking: use for documentation, structured MD. Pros: preserves structure. Cons: size variance. Settings: split by #, ##, ###.
rule.rationale: Markdown headers are explicit structure; respecting them preserves the author's intended chunking.

rule.statement: Basic retrieval: embed query, find top-k similar chunks. Settings: top_k=5, cosine similarity.
rule.rationale: Simple and fast. Suitable for high-recall queries or small corpora where top-k is sufficient.

rule.statement: Hybrid search: combine vector + keyword search. Best for exact match + semantic. Settings: 70% vector, 30% BM25.
rule.rationale: Vector search misses exact technical terms; BM25 catches them. Hybrid balances recall for both semantic and exact queries.

rule.statement: Reranking: retrieve top-20, rerank, return top-5. Best for high precision needs. Models: Cohere rerank, cross-encoders.
rule.rationale: Vector search similarity is calibrated for recall. A cross-encoder reranks for precision, improving the quality of the final context.

rule.statement: Query enhancement methods: query expansion (vague questions → better recall), HyDE/hypothetical answer (abstract questions → better semantic match), query rewriting (ambiguous questions → clarity).
rule.rationale: Reformulating the query can improve retrieval when the original phrasing is too different from the corpus language.

rule.statement: Context window strategies: minimal (3 chunks, 1500 tokens; simple Q&A), standard (5 chunks, 2500 tokens; general RAG), rich (10 chunks, 5000 tokens; complex reasoning), extended (20 chunks, 10000 tokens; research).
rule.rationale: Larger context windows improve coverage but risk context overflow. Tailor to query complexity and model limits.

rule.statement: Context ordering: by relevance (most relevant first, default), by recency (newest first for time-sensitive), by source (group by document for navigation).
rule.rationale: Relevance-first aligns with model attention patterns. Recency can override relevance for time-sensitive queries. Source grouping helps humans navigate retrieved content.

rule.statement: Avoid context overflow: top_k * avg_chunk_tokens must stay well below context limit after system prompt and examples.
rule.rationale: Context overflow causes the model to drop earlier chunks, likely losing important context.

rule.statement: Qdrant: recommended for production self-hosted. High performance, cloud-native, rich filtering, Docker deployment, free and open-source.
rule.rationale: Qdrant offers the best balance of performance, filtering, and self-hosting flexibility. Recommended for production systems with control requirements.

rule.statement: Weaviate: knowledge graphs, strong for multi-tenancy. Free and open-source. Kubernetes deployable. Built-in hybrid search and BM25.
rule.rationale: Weaviate's knowledge graph model fits systems where relationships between documents matter. Multi-tenancy support aids SaaS deployments.

rule.statement: Chroma: best for local dev, prototyping. Embedded database, no external dependencies, persistent or in-memory, free and open-source.
rule.rationale: Chroma's embedded model eliminates DevOps burden during prototyping. Perfect for notebook-based development.

rule.statement: pgvector: add vector search to existing Postgres. Free and open-source. Avoids new infrastructure. Uses psycopg2 or asyncpg.
rule.rationale: If Postgres is already in the stack, pgvector avoids adding a new service. Trade-off: less powerful filtering than specialized vector DBs.

rule.statement: Pinecone: fully managed, serverless tier, no self-hosting. SaaS with cost at scale. Supports 1B+ vectors.
rule.rationale: Pinecone eliminates operational overhead but costs more at scale. Best for teams without DevOps resources or with bursty query patterns.

rule.statement: Retrieval quality: recall@k (relevant docs in top-k), precision@k (top-k are relevant), MRR (mean reciprocal rank), NDCG (normalized discounted cumulative gain).
rule.rationale: Separate retrieval quality from generation to identify where the pipeline breaks. Recall measures coverage; precision measures accuracy.

rule.statement: Generation quality: faithfulness (response grounded in context), relevance (answers the question), completeness (covers key points), conciseness (not overly verbose).
rule.rationale: Good generation cannot compensate for poor retrieval. Measure faithfulness to catch hallucinations.

rule.statement: End-to-end: accuracy (correct answers), latency (response time), cost (API + DB costs), user satisfaction (feedback scores).
rule.rationale: These metrics tie back to user experience and business goals.

rule.statement: Chunking: match chunk size to question complexity. Use overlap (10-20%) to maintain context. Consider document structure. Test different strategies.
rule.rationale: Empirical tuning is required; no one-size-fits-all defaults exist.

rule.statement: Retrieval: start with more chunks (top-k=10), filter down. Use hybrid search (vector + keyword). Implement reranking for precision. Cache embeddings for performance.
rule.rationale: This progression moves from recall to precision while maintaining performance.

rule.statement: Context: don't overwhelm LLM with too much context. Order chunks by relevance. Include source metadata. Remove duplicate information.
rule.rationale: Excessive context hurts generation quality. Metadata enables citations. Duplicates waste context budget.

rule.statement: Prompts: be explicit about using only provided context. Include instructions for handling missing info. Request citations when needed. Test with diverse questions.
rule.rationale: Explicit constraints prevent hallucination. Citations enable verification. Diverse tests expose failures.

rule.statement: Evaluation: test with diverse questions. Measure retrieval and generation separately. Track faithfulness and relevance. Iterate based on metrics.
rule.rationale: Separate measurement enables root-cause analysis. Iteration based on data beats guessing.

rule.statement: Chunks too large: LLM can't focus. Solution: reduce to 500 chars. Chunks too small: loss of context. Solution: increase to 300+ chars.
rule.rationale: Optimal size is corpus-dependent. Iterative tuning is required.

rule.statement: Wrong embedding model: poor retrieval. Solution: match embedding model to use case. No overlap: split information. Solution: add 10-20% overlap.
rule.rationale: Embedding model choice affects all downstream retrieval. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries.

rule.statement: Ignoring metadata: missing filters. Solution: add source, date, type. Static top-k: over/under retrieval. Solution: adapt per query type.
rule.rationale: Metadata enables intelligent filtering. Adaptive retrieval balances recall vs. precision per query.

rule.statement: No reranking: low precision. Solution: add reranking step. Index goes stale without incremental updates: answers reference outdated information. Solution: design streaming update strategy.
rule.rationale: Reranking is essential for production. Stale indexes are a silent failure mode; proactive update design prevents it.

rule.statement: Agentic RAG: agent decides when to retrieve. Multiple retrieval rounds. Dynamic query formulation.
rule.rationale: For multi-hop reasoning, a single retrieval round is insufficient. Agents can iterate, refining queries based on intermediate results.

rule.statement: Graph RAG: combine vector + knowledge graph. Entity relationships. Multi-hop reasoning.
rule.rationale: Knowledge graphs capture relationships that vector search alone misses, enabling structured reasoning.

rule.statement: Multi-index RAG: separate indexes per domain. Route queries to appropriate index. Combine results from multiple sources.
rule.rationale: Domain-specific indexes can use different chunking strategies and embedding models, optimizing each independently.

rule.statement: Faithfulness breaks when retrieval returns off-topic chunks and LLM generates from prior knowledge instead. Static top-k retrieval causes both over-retrieval (noisy context) and under-retrieval (missing key chunks) for queries of varying complexity.
rule.rationale: These are architectural, not just prompt-engineering, issues. Require iterative retrieval or adaptive top-k.

rule.statement: Context ordering matters but is often ignored. Most relevant chunk should appear first; LLMs show recency bias. Hybrid search requires both vector and keyword index; schema changes must be synchronized across both.
rule.rationale: LLM attention patterns are empirical facts. Hybrid indexing doubles operational complexity; only use if hybrid retrieval is necessary.

rule.statement: Prompt templates that don't explicitly forbid hallucination produce grounded-sounding but invented answers. Latency budget must account for embedding + vector search + (optional rerank) + LLM generation — each stage can independently spike.
rule.rationale: Hallucination is hard to detect; explicit constraints + post-checks are required. Latency is multi-stage; measure end-to-end, not just LLM generation.

rule.statement: Analyze and assess: Sonnet (evaluation and planning). Execute implementation: Haiku (apply established patterns). Review and validate: Sonnet (quality assurance). Strategic decision: Opus (novel scenarios). Optimize and refine: Haiku (performance tuning). Document approach: Haiku (create documentation).
rule.rationale: Sonnet balances reasoning and cost for planning and review. Haiku is efficient for routine execution and documentation. Opus handles novel decisions.

rule.statement: Default system prompt: "You are a helpful assistant that answers questions based on context. Rules: Answer based ONLY on provided context. If context doesn't contain answer, say 'I don't have enough information'. Be concise and accurate."
rule.rationale: This default balances clarity, constraint enforcement, and fallback messaging. Suitable for most Q&A scenarios.

rule.statement: Technical documentation prompt: "You are a technical documentation assistant. Rules: Provide accurate, detailed technical answers. Include code examples when relevant. Reference specific documentation sections. Explain technical concepts clearly."
rule.rationale: Technical contexts benefit from explicit instruction to cite sections and provide examples.

rule.statement: Citation style prompt: "You are a research assistant that provides cited answers. Rules: Always cite sources using [Source: X] format. Quote relevant passages when helpful. Distinguish between direct quotes and paraphrasing. Note when sources conflict."
rule.rationale: Citation-focused prompts enable compliance and user verification. Distinction between quotes and paraphrasing maintains research integrity.

rule.statement: Calculate token budget: reserve system prompt (500-1000 tokens), few-shot examples if any (500-2000 tokens), user query (100-500 tokens), retrieved context (remaining budget). Reserve 20% for generation headroom.
rule.rationale: Explicit budgeting prevents context overflow and ensures space for the model's response.

rule.statement: For Opus (200k context): standard RAG with 10-20 chunks (2500-5000 tokens), rich context with multiple evidence strands. For Sonnet (200k context): same as Opus. For Haiku (200k context): focus on precision; rerank aggressively to top 3-5 chunks.
rule.rationale: All three Claude models have large context windows, but latency and cost tradeoffs suggest aggressive filtering for Haiku.

rule.statement: Token counting: use tiktoken library to count prompt + context tokens before inference. If total exceeds model limit minus 2000 (safety margin), reduce top_k or rerank more aggressively.
rule.rationale: Actual token counts vary by model; tiktoken provides accuracy. Safety margin prevents spillover to truncation.

rule.statement: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (3072-dim): industry standard for general Q&A, strong on semantic search, 2M token batch limit, $0.13 per 1M tokens. For cost-sensitive: text-embedding-3-small (1536-dim), 25% cheaper.
rule.rationale: text-embedding-3-large is the default choice unless cost is a constraint. Smaller model trades quality for latency and cost.

rule.statement: Voyage AI voyage-3 (1024-dim): strong alternative, specialized for domain-specific corpora, similar cost. For open-source: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384-dim), free, good for small corpora, weaker on long documents.
rule.rationale: Voyage's specialization helps on niche domains. Open-source options reduce API dependency but lower retrieval quality.

rule.statement: Match embedding model to retrieval task: semantic Q&A → large (3072-dim). Factual lookups → hybrid (vector + BM25) with smaller model. Code search → specialized embeddings or BM25 only.
rule.rationale: Embedding quality directly affects all downstream retrieval. Tailor model to query type.

rule.statement: Initial indexing: batch embed documents in chunks of 50-100 (respect API rate limits). Store chunk metadata: source, chunk_index, page, ingestion_date, source_id. Use a staging index during ingestion, flip to production after validation.
rule.rationale: Batching respects rate limits and reduces API cost. Metadata enables filtering and citations. Staging prevents index corruption.

rule.statement: Incremental updates: track document hash (MD5 or SHA256 of source file). On update, compare hash. If different, soft-delete old chunks (mark is_deleted=true, don't physically remove) and insert new chunks with updated chunk indices.
rule.rationale: Hash comparison identifies changed files in O(n). Soft-delete enables rollback. New chunk indices maintain referential integrity.

rule.statement: Index freshness: for frequently-changing corpora (news, documentation), schedule daily re-indexing. For static corpora, re-index on explicit updates only. Monitor index age in retrieval responses; alert if age exceeds SLA.
rule.rationale: Index age affects answer recency. Monitoring prevents silent staleness.
